---
title: "31. AI Disclosure and Research Integrity"
---

[![Open the lab in Colab](https://colab.research.google.com/assets/colab-badge.svg)](https://colab.research.google.com/github/davi-moreira/2026F_evidence_driven_research_purdue_HONR464/blob/main/notebooks/student/nb12_conference_student.ipynb){target="_blank"}

> **The research decision.** Decide exactly what to disclose about every AI tool you
> used on your project (which tool, for which task, and how you checked its output),
> and record it so that when someone asks *"did you use AI, and how did you verify
> it?"* you can answer truthfully and point to a checkable record, not a memory.

## Why this decision matters

> *"I am not going to penalize you for using an AI tool. I am going to penalize you
> for not being able to tell me where you used it and how you checked it. A result I
> cannot trace back to a source and a verification is a result I cannot trust."*
> — a **conference reviewer**, standing at your poster and asking the AI-use question

At the Expo, one of the five kinds of question a stranger can ask you is the AI-use
question. Many journals and conferences now ask authors to state where and how AI
tools were used, so this is not a classroom formality. If your only answer is "yes, a
bit, and it seemed fine," you have turned good work into an untrustable result on the
spot. The disclosure is what keeps an honest use of AI honest under questioning.

## The concept

**AI disclosure** is a truthful statement of where and how you used an AI tool in
your work. Example: "I used Gemini to draft the code that computed my safety factor,
and I recomputed that number by hand before reporting it." **Research integrity** is
the practice of keeping your work honest and traceable, so a reader can see where each
result came from and trust it. Example: a claim that names its source and its check
has integrity; a claim that names neither does not.

The tool that makes disclosure easy is the one you have kept all semester: the **AI
Research Ledger**, a running table with one row per AI use, recording the task, the
tool, your decision, and the verification method. A complete disclosure is not written
from scratch at the end. It is read straight off your ledger.

Three parts make a disclosure complete, and each maps to a ledger column: **which
tool** you used, **for which task**, and **how you verified** the output. Drop any one
and it fails. "I used AI" with no task is meaningless; a task with no verification is
exactly the gap a reviewer is trained to find.

## A worked example

You are presenting a senior-design poster in mechanical engineering. Your bracket
must hold a static design load, and your headline number is its **safety factor**,
the ratio of the material's strength to the stress your load actually puts on the
part. A safety factor of 2.0 means the part can take twice the load before it yields.

You used an AI tool twice: once to find the yield strength of 6061-T6 aluminum, once
to write the short calculation that turns that strength and your load into a safety
factor. Both are honest uses, and neither is safe until it is disclosed and verified.

Your ledger already holds two rows. For the material property, the verification method
is **primary-source reading**: you opened the alloy's published datasheet and confirmed
the yield strength the tool gave you. For the calculation, the method is **alternative
code**, recomputing the same number a second way:

```python
# The number the AI code reported, recomputed by hand.
yield_strength = 276      # MPa, confirmed against the 6061-T6 datasheet
applied_stress = 131      # MPa, from my load and cross-section
safety_factor  = yield_strength / applied_stress
print(round(safety_factor, 2))   # 2.11 — matches the AI-generated cell
```

When the reviewer asks the AI-use question, you do not improvise. You read your two
rows aloud: the tools, the tasks, and the two checks. The claim you defend is bounded.
The safety factor is about 2.1 under this static load, with the strength confirmed
against the datasheet and the arithmetic reproduced by hand. Its boundary is honest
too: you modeled a single idealized load case, not fatigue and not stress
concentration at the holes.

## The Colab laboratory

This chapter's lab is notebook **nb12 — Final conference preparation, presentation + reflection**. Open it in Colab with the
badge above and work the cells alongside this chapter. There you code the AI-use
questions you were asked as data and fill the AI Research Ledger that becomes the
disclosure you defend. The final Exit Defense asks you to name what you delegated to
Gemini and the specific check that decided whether you accepted, changed, or rejected
it.

## Recommended AI prompts

Commit your own answer first, then delegate. Each prompt is a checkable job, not a
request for a verdict.

**Locate the standard.**

```text
Act as a research-integrity assistant. Name the elements a complete AI-use
disclosure should contain for a student research poster, and cite one real,
retrievable source such as a university research-integrity office or a major
publisher's AI policy. Only name a source you are confident exists.
```
After running, verify: open the cited policy and confirm each named element is
actually in it. Counters *confident fabrication* (an invented policy element arrives
as confidently as a real one).

**Turn notes into a disclosure table to verify.**

```text
Here are my raw notes on every time I used an AI tool on this project: [paste].
Build a table with columns tool, task, and verification method, one row per use.
Do not invent a verification I did not mention. If a use has no check, leave that
cell blank and flag the row.
```
After running, verify: match each row against your ledger; a verification filled in
where your notes had none is fabricated, so cut it. Counters *illusion of
completeness* (a tidy table that quietly reports checks you never ran).

**Red-team your disclosure.**

```text
Here is my drafted AI-use disclosure: [paste]. Act as a hostile reviewer who
suspects I am hiding a use or overstating my checks. Name every place I claim a
verification I have not shown, or omit a use a reviewer would expect. Do not
rewrite it for me.
```
After running, verify: for each flagged line, produce the ledger row that backs it or
delete the claim. Counters *sycophantic agreement* (a reviewer that praises your
honesty instead of testing it).

::: {.callout-important title="Do not delegate"}
The honesty of the disclosure is yours alone. You decide **which uses to disclose**
(including the one you would rather not mention), **whether a verification you claim
actually happened**, and **whether the final statement is true**. A tool can format
your ledger into a clean paragraph. It cannot know what you really did or certify that
you checked it. Signing your name to a disclosure you cannot back is the integrity
failure this chapter exists to prevent.
:::

## An AI failure case

You ask a tool to "write my AI-use disclosure from these notes," and it hands back a
polished paragraph: *"All AI-generated outputs were independently verified against
primary sources."* It reads beautifully, and it is false. You verified the yield
strength against the datasheet, but you never independently checked the load figure the
same tool helped you estimate. The sentence upgraded one real check into a blanket
claim of "all."

You catch it by reading the disclosure line by line against your ledger. Every
sentence claiming a verification must point to a row that names that check. The word
"all" has no such row, so it is fabricated and comes out. Your true disclosure is
narrower and stronger: you name the two checks you ran and the one input you did not
independently confirm. A confident paragraph is not a truthful one. You verify the
disclosure against the record, not against how responsible it sounds.

## Verification laboratory

Verify this chapter's key result, your finished disclosure, with **peer reasoning**
from the [verification guide](https://github.com/davi-moreira/2026F_evidence_driven_research_purdue_HONR464/blob/main/ai_resources/verification_guide.md).
Hand a partner your disclosure and your actual project, and ask one question: can you
find an AI use I did not disclose, or a verification I claimed but cannot show? If they
find one, your disclosure was incomplete, which is a finding, not a failure. For the
engineering number itself, recompute the safety factor a second way, so the value you
disclose survives two independent roads to the same answer.

## Project transfer

This chapter turns your AI Research Ledger into the disclosure you submit and defend
with **M12**, your conference reflection and criticism portfolio. The ledger is a
required part of that package: a missing ledger scores Craft 0 and the submission is
returned unread. So read your finished ledger into a short, truthful AI-use statement,
attach it to M12, and rehearse answering the Expo's AI-use question straight from it.
When a stranger asks how you checked your work, your answer is already written and
already verified.

## Defend your decision

In four sentences, defend your disclosure. Name one AI use on your project and the
exact verification you ran on it. State the bounded claim your disclosure lets you
make, with its boundary: what your check does *not* establish. Close with your
uncertainty, the one AI-assisted input you have not yet independently confirmed and
the check you would run next to close the gap.
